info.title: **OpenCode slow to open — investigating startup performance**

parts.text: Why is my opencode taking so long to open now?

**Diagnosing plan mode**

I need to answer and diagnose the current situation. The first step is to check if I'm in read-only mode. Should I mention that? I'll need to inspect further and consider if I should use my skills to customize. It seems like I might want to look into the open-code configuration. The idea of loading something comes to mind too—what could that entail? Let’s clarify this step by step!
I’ll inspect the OpenCode config and startup hooks read-only, then correlate recent changes with anything that runs during launch.

state.output:
<skill_content name="customize-opencode">
# Skill: customize-opencode

<!--
  Built-in skill. Name and description are registered in code at
  packages/opencode/src/skill/index.ts (see CUSTOMIZE_OPENCODE_SKILL_NAME
  and CUSTOMIZE_OPENCODE_SKILL_DESCRIPTION). The body below becomes the
  skill's content.
-->

# Customizing opencode

opencode validates its own config strictly and refuses to start when a field
is wrong. The shapes below cover the common surface area, but they are a
**summary, not the source of truth**.

## Full schema reference

The authoritative list of every config option — with field types, enums,
defaults, and descriptions — lives in the published JSON Schema:

**<https://opencode.ai/config.json>**

If a field is not documented in this skill, or you need to confirm an exact
shape before writing config, **fetch that URL and read the schema directly**
rather than guessing. opencode hard-fails on invalid config, so the cost of a
wrong shape is a broken startup.

Independently, every `opencode.json` should declare
`"$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json"` so the user's editor catches
mistakes as they type.

## Applying changes

Config is loaded once when opencode starts and is not hot-reloaded. After
saving changes to `opencode.json`, an agent file, a skill, a plugin, or any
other config-time file, **tell the user to quit and restart opencode** for
the changes to take effect. The running session will keep using the
already-loaded config until then.

## Where files live

| Scope                         | Path                                                                                                                      |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Project config                | `./opencode.json`, `./opencode.jsonc`, or `.opencode/opencode.json` (opencode walks up from the cwd to the worktree root) |
| Global config                 | `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json` (NOT `~/.opencode/`)                                                                   |
| Project agents                | `.opencode/agent/<name>.md` or `.opencode/agents/<name>.md`                                                               |
| Global agents                 | `~/.config/opencode/agent(s)/<name>.md`                                                                                   |
| Project skills                | `.opencode/skill(s)/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                                                      |
| Global skills                 | `~/.config/opencode/skill(s)/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                                             |
| External skills (auto-loaded) | `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, `~/.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`                                                    |

Configs from each scope are deep-merged. Project overrides global. Unknown
top-level keys in `opencode.json` are rejected with `ConfigInvalidError`.

## opencode.json

Every field is optional.

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
  "username": "string",
  "model": "provider/model-id",
  "small_model": "provider/model-id",
  "default_agent": "agent-name",
  "shell": "/bin/zsh",
  "logLevel": "DEBUG" | "INFO" | "WARN" | "ERROR",
  "share": "manual" | "auto" | "disabled",
  "autoupdate": true | false | "notify",
  "snapshot": true,
  "instructions": ["AGENTS.md", "docs/style.md"],

  "skills": {
    "paths": [".opencode/skills", "/abs/path/to/skills"],
    "urls": ["https://example.com/.well-known/skills/"]
  },

  "agent": {
    "my-agent": {
      "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "mode": "subagent",
      "description": "...",
      "permission": { "edit": "deny" }
    }
  },

  "command": {
    "deploy": { "description": "...", "prompt": "..." }
  },

  "provider": {
    "anthropic": { "options": { "apiKey": "..." } }
  },
  "disabled_providers": ["openai"],
  "enabled_providers": ["anthropic"],

  "mcp": {
    "playwright": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "@playwright/mcp"],
      "enabled": true,
      "env": {}
    },
    "remote-thing": {
      "type": "remote",
      "url": "https://...",
      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ..." }
    }
  },

  "plugin": [
    "opencode-gemini-auth",
    "opencode-foo@1.2.3",
    "./local-plugin.ts",
    ["opencode-bar", { "option": "value" }]
  ],

  "permission": {
    "edit": "deny",
    "bash": { "git *": "allow", "*": "ask" }
  },

  "formatter": false,
  "lsp": false,

  "experimental": {
    "primary_tools": ["edit"],
    "mcp_timeout": 30000
  },

  "tool_output": { "max_lines": 200, "max_bytes": 8192 },

  "compaction": { "auto": true, "tail_turns": 15 }
}
```

Shape notes worth being explicit about:

- `model` always carries a provider prefix: `"anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"`.
- `skills` is an object with `paths` and/or `urls`, not an array.
- `agent` is an object keyed by agent name, not an array.
- `plugin` is an array of strings or `[name, options]` tuples, not an object.
- `mcp[name].command` is an array of strings, never a single string. `type` is required.
- `permission` is either a string action or an object keyed by tool name.

## Skills

opencode's skill loader scans for `**/SKILL.md` inside skill directories. The
file is named `SKILL.md` exactly, and lives in its own folder named after the
skill:

```
.opencode/skills/my-skill/SKILL.md
```

Frontmatter:

```markdown
---
name: my-skill
description: One sentence covering what this skill does AND when to trigger it. Front-load the literal keywords or filenames the user is likely to say.
---

# My Skill

(skill body in markdown: instructions, examples, references)
```

- `name` is required, lowercase hyphen-separated, up to 64 chars, and matches the folder name.
- `description` is effectively required: skills without one are filtered out and never surfaced to the model. Cover both _what_ the skill does and _when_ to use it. Write in third person ("Use when...", not "I help with..."). Front-load concrete trigger keywords and filenames; gate with "Use ONLY when..." if the skill should stay quiet on adjacent topics.
- Optional: `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata` (string-string map).

Register skills from non-default locations via `skills.paths` (scanned
recursively for `**/SKILL.md`) and `skills.urls` (each URL serves a list of
skills).

## Agents

Two ways to define an agent. Use the file form for anything non-trivial.

### Inline (in `opencode.json`)

```json
{
  "agent": {
    "my-reviewer": {
      "description": "Reviews PRs for style violations.",
      "mode": "subagent",
      "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "permission": { "edit": "deny", "bash": "ask" },
      "prompt": "You are a strict PR reviewer..."
    }
  }
}
```

### File

```

**Inspecting files and commands**

I need to check some files now. I think I'll use tools like glob, read, and grep for this inspection. Additionally, I might want to look at the git status and log to see what's going on. Is bash readonly allowed for these commands? I'm considering all these options to ensure I have a clear picture of the files and their status. I should also think about using parallel tools if that helps speed things up!

import { existsSync, mkdtempSync, rmSync, writeFileSync } from "node:fs"
import { tmpdir } from "node:os"
import { join } from "node:path"
import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"

type HookResult = {
  stdout: string
  stderr: string
  exitCode: number
}

type ToolPayload = {
  tool_name: string
  tool_input: Record<string, unknown>
  tool_output?: string
  error?: string
  session_id?: string
}

const DOTFILES_ROOT = join(process.env.HOME || "", "dotfiles")

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: // OpenCode plugin: expose session metadata to shell commands.
2: // Tmux window colors are pane-local and owned by scripts/opencode/tmux-open.sh.
3: import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
4: 
5: export const SessionEnvPlugin: Plugin = async () => {
6:   let currentSessionID: string | null = null
7:   let currentModel: string | null = null
8: 
9:   return {
10:     event: async ({ event }) => {
11:       switch (event.type) {
12:         case "session.created": {
13:           const session = (event as any).properties?.info
14:           if (!session?.id) break
15:           currentSessionID = session.id
16:           break
17:         }
18: 
19:         case "message.updated": {
20:           const msg = (event as any).properties?.info
21:           if (msg?.role === "assistant" && msg?.modelID) {
22:             currentModel = msg.modelID
23:           }
24:           break
25:         }
26: 
27:         case "session.deleted": {
28:           currentSessionID = null
29:           currentModel = null
30:           break
31:         }
32: 
33:         case "server.instance.disposed": {
34:           // OpenCode can dispose transient server instances while the attached
35:           // TUI is still alive. Session metadata is cleared only on deletion.
36:           break
37:         }
38:       }
39:     },
40:     "shell.env": async (input, output) => {
41:       const sessionID = input.sessionID ?? currentSessionID
42:       if (sessionID) {
43:         currentSessionID = sessionID

1: // Entire CLI plugin for OpenCode
2: // Auto-generated by `entire enable --agent opencode`
3: // Do not edit manually — changes will be overwritten on next install.
4: // Requires Bun runtime (used by OpenCode's plugin system for loading ESM plugins).

6: 
7: export const EntirePlugin: Plugin = async ({ directory }) => {
8:   const ENTIRE_CMD = 'entire'
9:   // Track seen user messages to fire turn-start only once per message
10:   const seenUserMessages = new Set<string>()
11:   // Track current session ID for message events (which don't include sessionID)
12:   let currentSessionID: string | null = null
13:   // Track the model used by the most recent assistant message
14:   let currentModel: string | null = null
15:   // In-memory store for message metadata (role, tokens, etc.)
16:   const messageStore = new Map<string, any>()
17: 
18:   /**
19:    * Build the shell command for a hook invocation.
20:    * Uses sh -c so that shell command substitution in ENTIRE_CMD
21:    * (e.g., $(git rev-parse --show-toplevel) for local-dev) is interpreted.
22:    */
23:   function hookCmd(hookName: string): string[] {
24:     return ["sh", "-c", `${ENTIRE_CMD} hooks opencode ${hookName}`]
25:   }
26: 
27:   /**
28:    * Pipe JSON payload to an entire hooks command (async).
29:    * Errors are logged but never thrown — plugin failures must not crash OpenCode.
30:    */
31:   async function callHook(hookName: string, payload: Record<string, unknown>) {
32:     try {
33:       const json = JSON.stringify(payload)
34:       const proc = Bun.spawn(hookCmd(hookName), {
35:         cwd: directory,
36:         stdin: new Blob([json + "\n"]),
37:         stdout: "ignore",
38:         stderr: "ignore",
39:       })
40:       await proc.exited
41:     } catch {
42:       // Silently ignore — plugin failures must not crash OpenCode
43:     }
44:   }
45: 
46:   /**
47:    * Synchronous variant for hooks that fire near process exit (turn-end, session-end).
48:    * `opencode run` breaks its event loop on the same session.status idle event that
49:    * triggers turn-end. The async callHook would be killed before completing.
50:    * Bun.spawnSync blocks the event loop, preventing exit until the hook finishes.
51:    */
52:   function callHookSync(hookName: string, payload: Record<string, unknown>) {
53:     try {
54:       const json = JSON.stringify(payload)
55:       Bun.spawnSync(hookCmd(hookName), {
56:         cwd: directory,
57:         stdin: new TextEncoder().encode(json + "\n"),
58:         stdout: "ignore",
59:         stderr: "ignore",
60:       })
61:     } catch {
62:       // Silently ignore — plugin failures must not crash OpenCode
63:     }
64:   }
65: 
66:   return {
67:     event: async ({ event }) => {
68:       try {
69:         switch (event.type) {
70:           case "session.created": {
71:             const session = (event as any).properties?.info
72:             if (!session?.id) break
73:             // Reset per-session tracking state when switching sessions.
74:             if (currentSessionID !== session.id) {
75:               seenUserMessages.clear()
76:               messageStore.clear()
77:               currentModel = null
78:               await callHook("session-start", {
79:                 session_id: session.id,
80:               })
81:             }
82:             currentSessionID = session.id
83:             break
84:           }
85: 
86:           case "message.updated": {
87:             const msg = (event as any).properties?.info
88:             if (!msg) break
89:             // Store message metadata (role, time, tokens, etc.)
90:             messageStore.set(msg.id, msg)
91:             // Track model from assistant messages
92:             if (msg.role === "assistant" && msg.modelID) {
93:               currentModel = msg.modelID
94:             }
95:             break
96:           }
97: 
98:           case "message.part.updated": {
99:             const part = (event as any).properties?.part
100:             if (!part?.messageID) break
101: 
102:             // Fire turn-start on the first text part of a new user message
103:             const msg = messageStore.get(part.messageID)
104:             if (msg?.role === "user" && part.type === "text" && !seenUserMessages.has(msg.id)) {
105:               seenUserMessages.add(msg.id)
106:               const sessionID = msg.sessionID ?? currentSessionID
107:               if (sessionID) {
108:                 await callHook("turn-start", {
109:                   session_id: sessionID,
110:                   prompt: part.text ?? "",
111:                   model: currentModel ?? "",
112:                 })
113:               }
114:             }
115:             break
116:           }
117: 
118:           case "session.status": {
119:             // session.status fires in both TUI and non-interactive (run) mode.
120:             // session.idle is deprecated and not reliably emitted in run mode.
121:             const props = (event as any).properties
122:             if (props?.status?.type !== "idle") break
123:             const sessionID = props?.sessionID ?? currentSessionID
124:             if (!sessionID) break
125:             // Use sync variant: `opencode run` exits on the same idle event,
126:             // so an async hook would be killed before completing.
127:             callHookSync("turn-end", {
128:               session_id: sessionID,
129:               model: currentModel ?? "",
130:             })
131:             break
132:           }
133: 
134:           case "session.compacted": {
135:             const sessionID = (event as any).properties?.sessionID
136:             if (!sessionID) break

138:               session_id: sessionID,
139:             })
140:             break
141:           }
142: 
143:           case "session.deleted": {
144:             const session = (event as any).properties?.info
145:             if (!session?.id) break
146:             seenUserMessages.clear()
147:             messageStore.clear()
148:             currentSessionID = null
149:             // Use sync variant: session-end may fire during shutdown.
150:             callHookSync("session-end", {
151:               session_id: session.id,
152:             })
153:             break
154:           }
155: 
156:           case "server.instance.disposed": {
157:             // Fires when OpenCode shuts down (TUI close or `opencode run` exit).
158:             // session.deleted only fires on explicit user deletion, not on quit,
159:             // so this is the only reliable way to end sessions on exit.
160:             if (!currentSessionID) break
161:             const sessionID = currentSessionID
162:             seenUserMessages.clear()
163:             messageStore.clear()
164:             currentSessionID = null
165:             // Use sync variant: this is the last event before process exit.
166:             callHookSync("session-end", {
167:               session_id: sessionID,
168:             })
169:             break
170:           }
171:         }
172:       } catch {
173:         // Silently ignore — plugin failures must not crash OpenCode
174:       }
175:     },
176:   }
177: }

677:       await logToolFailure(input.tool.toLowerCase(), args, output?.error || output?.message || "Tool execution failed")
678:     },
679: 
680:     "experimental.chat.system.transform": async (_input, output) => {
681:       for (const text of sessionContext) {
682:         output.system.push(text)
683:       }
684: 
685:       while (transientContext.length > 0) {
686:         const next = transientContext.shift()
687:         if (next) {
688:           output.system.push(next)
689:         }
690:       }
691:     },
692: 
693:     "experimental.session.compacting": async (_input, output) => {
694:       try {
695:         appendCompactContext(output, runCommandSync("bd", ["prime"]))
696:       } catch {
697:         // bd may be unavailable in minimal test or bootstrap environments.
698:       }
699: 
700:       appendCompactContext(output, runScriptSync(hookPath("plan-persist.sh")))
701:       appendCompactContext(output, runScriptSync(hookPath("changelog-persist.sh")))
702:     },
703:   }
704: }

13:   }

16:   if (existsSync(profilesFile)) {

18:   }

26: 
27:   if (!process.env.MERIDIAN_DEFAULT_PROFILE) {
28:     process.env.MERIDIAN_DEFAULT_PROFILE = "default"
29:   }
30: }
31: 
32: async function loadSharedPlugin(input: Parameters<Plugin>[0]) {
33:   if (!sharedPluginPromise) {
34:     sharedPluginPromise = (async () => {
35:       const home = process.env.HOME
36:       if (!home) {
37:         throw new Error("HOME is required to load opencode-with-claude")
38:       }
39: 
40:       seedMeridianProfile(home)
41: 
42:       // OpenCode can initialize plugins multiple times in one process while it
43:       // bootstraps worker/session phases. Reuse a single upstream plugin
44:       // instance so Meridian only starts one Claude proxy per run.
45:       const modulePath = `${home}/.bun/install/global/node_modules/opencode-with-claude/dist/index.js`
46:       const { ClaudeMaxPlugin } = (await import(modulePath)) as ClaudePluginModule
47:       return ClaudeMaxPlugin(input)
48:     })().catch((error) => {
49:       sharedPluginPromise = null
50:       throw error
51:     })
52:   }
53: 
54:   return sharedPluginPromise
55: }

58:   return loadSharedPlugin(input)
59: }

**Inspecting operational modes**

I need to figure out if actual timing is allowed. It seems like running opencode might not be in read-only mode, since it starts the service and possibly modifies logs. I think that plan mode prohibits system changes, which is important to note. I could run opencode with the --help option, but I'm not sure if that will start anything. Honestly, I should inspect the logs in read-only mode and look for the necessary log files.

1: #!/bin/bash
2: set -euo pipefail
3: # SessionStart hook: Report available LSP servers to Claude Code context
4: # This injects a system message so Claude knows which LSP capabilities are active.
5: # Lightweight: only checks PATH for binaries, no network calls.
6: 
7: # LSP binary → language mapping
8: declare -A LSP_BINS=(
9:     ["pyright-langserver"]="Python"
10:     ["typescript-language-server"]="TypeScript/JavaScript"
11:     ["gopls"]="Go"
12:     ["rust-analyzer"]="Rust"
13:     ["bash-language-server"]="Bash/Shell"
14:     ["yaml-language-server"]="YAML"
15:     ["terraform-ls"]="Terraform"
16:     ["lua-language-server"]="Lua"
17:     ["nil"]="Nix"
18: )
19: 
20: available=()
21: for bin in "${!LSP_BINS[@]}"; do
22:     if command -v "$bin" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
23:         available+=("${LSP_BINS[$bin]}")
24:     fi
25: done
26: 
27: # Only output if at least one LSP is available
28: if [ ${#available[@]} -gt 0 ]; then
29:     # Sort for consistent output
30:     IFS=$'\n' sorted=($(sort <<<"${available[*]}"))
31:     unset IFS
32: 
33:     echo "LSP servers active: ${sorted[*]}. Use the LSP tool for code intelligence (goToDefinition, findReferences, hover, documentSymbol, workspaceSymbol)."
34: fi
35: 
36: exit 0

<system-reminder>
Instructions from: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.claude/AGENTS.md
# AGENTS.md - Claude Code Agent System Reference

Specialized agent system for Claude Code with domain, project-specific, and compact-output subagent files.

## Shell Reminder

- Fish is the primary shell for this machine.
- Do not suggest `unset` for interactive shell usage; use `set -e VAR` in Fish.
- `env -u VAR command` is acceptable for one-off commands started from Fish.
- If an example requires Bash or Zsh syntax, label it explicitly.

## Overview

Each agent is a **Claude Code subagent file** (`.claude/agents/*.md`) with YAML frontmatter defining name, description, tool access, model, and optional features. Claude auto-delegates to subagents based on their description fields. Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt.

**Core Features**:
- **Subagent Files**: Markdown files with YAML frontmatter in `.claude/agents/`
- **Auto-Delegation**: Claude uses description fields to decide when to delegate
- **Tool Restrictions**: Each agent has specific tool access (read-only vs full)
- **Model Selection**: `haiku` for fast read-only agents, `inherit` for full capability
- **Persistent Memory**: `memory: project` on architect for cross-session learning
- **Background Mode**: `background: true` on test-runner for concurrent execution
- **Cost Control**: `maxTurns` on bounded agents (test-runner, dotfiles-doctor, mentor)
- **Skill Preloading**: `skills` field injects skill content at startup (shell-expert)
- **MCP Access**: `mcpServers: deepwiki` on architect and mentor for repo documentation
- **Lifecycle Hooks**: SubagentStart/SubagentStop events in settings.json for logging
- **Manual Override**: Use `--persona-[name]` flags for explicit control
- **Flag Integration**: Works with all thinking flags, MCP servers, and command categories

**Frontmatter Fields** (from [official docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)):

| Field | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | Yes | Agent identifier (must match filename) |
| `description` | Yes | When to delegate (Claude reads this) |
| `tools` | No | Comma-separated allowed tools |
| `disallowedTools` | No | Comma-separated blocked tools |
| `model` | No | `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, or `inherit` (default) |
| `memory` | No | `user`, `project`, or `local` persistence scope |
| `background` | No | `true` for concurrent execution |
| `maxTurns` | No | Limit API round-trips (cost control) |
| `skills` | No | Comma-separated skills to preload |
| `mcpServers` | No | Comma-separated MCP servers to enable |
| `hooks` | No | Agent-scoped hooks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop) |
| `isolation` | No | `worktree` for isolated git worktree |
| `permissionMode` | No | Permission level override |

## Available Agents

### Technical Specialists

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [architect](agents/architect.md) | `--persona-architect` | Systems architecture specialist | System design, scalability, long-term planning |
| [frontend](agents/frontend.md) | `--persona-frontend` | UX specialist & accessibility advocate | UI components, responsive design, user experience |
| [backend](agents/backend.md) | `--persona-backend` | Reliability engineer & API specialist | Server development, APIs, data integrity |
| [security](agents/security.md) | `--persona-security` | Threat modeler & vulnerability specialist | Security audits, threat analysis, hardening |
| [performance](agents/performance.md) | `--persona-performance` | Optimization & bottleneck specialist | Performance tuning, metrics, optimization |

### Process & Quality Experts

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [analyzer](agents/analyzer.md) | `--persona-analyzer` | Root cause & investigation specialist | Debugging, troubleshooting, analysis |
| [qa](agents/qa.md) | `--persona-qa` | Quality advocate & testing specialist | Testing, validation, quality assurance |
| [refactorer](agents/refactorer.md) | `--persona-refactorer` | Code quality & technical debt manager | Code cleanup, refactoring, simplification |
| [devops](agents/devops.md) | `--persona-devops` | Infrastructure & automation specialist | CI/CD, deployment, infrastructure |
| [devops-security-auditor](agents/devops-security-auditor.md) | Special | DevOps security assessment specialist | Infrastructure security, container security |

### Knowledge & Communication

| Agent | Flag | Description | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| [mentor](agents/mentor.md) | `--persona-mentor` | Educational & knowledge transfer specialist | Teaching, documentation, explanations |
| [scribe](agents/scribe.md) | `--persona-scribe=lang` | Documentation & localization specialist | Professional writing, documentation, translation |

### Project-Specific Agents

| Agent | Model | Extra Features | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| [shell-expert](agents/shell-expert.md) | inherit | `skills: fish-reload, dotfiles-sync` | Shell functions, scripts, Fish/Zsh parity |
| [test-runner](agents/test-runner.md) | haiku | `background: true`, `maxTurns: 10` | Running test suites, reporting results |
| [dotfiles-doctor](agents/dotfiles-doctor.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 15` | Stow validation, tool checks, theme consistency |

### Compact Caveman Agents

Use these when the task has a known shape and the parent context needs operational signal without prose. Do not use them for open-ended architecture, security threat modeling, mentoring, or requirements discovery.

| Agent | Model | Extra Features | Primary Use Cases |
|-------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| [caveman-investigator](agents/caveman-investigator.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 8` | Root-cause evidence, file location, compact debug handoff |
| [caveman-reviewer](agents/caveman-reviewer.md) | inherit | `maxTurns: 10` | Findings-only review with severity, file:line, fix |
| [caveman-tester](agents/caveman-tester.md) | haiku | `background: true`, `maxTurns: 8` | Targeted validation receipts |
| [caveman-builder](agents/caveman-builder.md) | inherit | `maxTurns: 12` | Surgical execution of already-defined small changes |
| [caveman-checklist](agents/caveman-checklist.md) | haiku | `maxTurns: 6` | Terse execution checklist for already-scoped work |

## Agent Activation

### Manual Activation
Use flags to explicitly activate agents:
```bash
--persona-architect           # Activate architect agent
--persona-frontend --persona-qa  # Activate multiple agents
--persona-scribe=es          # Activate scribe with Spanish language
```

### Auto-Activation
Agents activate automatically based on:
- **Keywords**: Domain-specific terms trigger relevant agents
- **Context**: Project type and current task determine agent selection
- **Commands**: Specific commands have preferred agents
- **Complexity**: High complexity triggers analytical agents

### Command Integration
Commands work seamlessly with agents:
```bash
/build --persona-frontend     # Frontend-focused build
/analyze                       # Auto-activates analyzer, architect, security
/improve --persona-refactorer # Refactoring-focused improvements
/test --persona-qa            # QA-focused testing
```

## Cross-Agent Collaboration

Agents can work together for complex tasks:

### Common Collaboration Patterns
- **architect + performance**: System design with performance optimization
- **security + backend**: Secure server-side development
- **frontend + qa**: User-focused development with testing
- **mentor + scribe**: Educational content creation
- **analyzer + refactorer**: Root cause analysis with code improvement
- **devops + security**: Infrastructure automation with security

### Conflict Resolution
When multiple agents are active:
1. **Primary Agent**: Leads decision-making within domain
2. **Consulting Agents**: Provide specialized input
3. **Validation Agents**: Review decisions for quality
4. **Priority Matrix**: Resolves conflicts using agent hierarchies

## Flag Integration

All agents work with the complete flag system:

### Thinking Flags
- `--think`: Enhanced analysis (4K tokens)
- `--think-hard`: Deep analysis (10K tokens)
- `--ultrathink`: Maximum analysis (32K tokens)

### MCP Server Flags
- `--seq`: Sequential reasoning
- `--c7`: Context7 documentation
- `--magic`: UI component generation
- `--play`: Playwright testing

### Optimization Flags
- `--uc`: Ultra-compressed output
- `--validate`: Pre-operation validation
- `--safe-mode`: Conservative execution
- `--loop`: Iterative improvement

### Orchestration Flags
- `--wave-mode`: Multi-stage execution
- `--delegate`: Sub-agent delegation
- `--concurrency`: Parallel processing

## Agent Selection Guide

### By Task Type
- **Building**: frontend, backend, architect
- **Debugging**: analyzer, qa, performance
- **Security**: security, devops-security-auditor
- **Documentation**: scribe, mentor
- **Optimization**: performance, refactorer
- **Infrastructure**: devops, architect

### By Project Phase
- **Planning**: architect, analyzer
- **Implementation**: frontend, backend
- **Testing**: qa, performance
- **Deployment**: devops, security
- **Maintenance**: refactorer, analyzer

### By Complexity
- **Simple tasks**: Single specialized agent
- **Moderate tasks**: Primary + consulting agent
- **Complex tasks**: Multiple collaborative agents
- **Critical tasks**: Full agent team with validation

## Configuration

Agents respect all system configurations:
- Token limits and compression settings
- MCP server availability
- Resource management thresholds
- Quality gate requirements
- Validation cycles

## Best Practices

1. **Let auto-activation work**: The system usually selects the right agents
2. **Use explicit flags for precision**: Override when you need specific expertise
3. **Combine agents for complex tasks**: Multiple perspectives improve outcomes
4. **Trust agent expertise**: Each agent excels in their domain
5. **Monitor agent recommendations**: Agents provide domain-specific insights

## Quick Reference

```bash
# Single agent activation
--persona-frontend

# Multiple agents
--persona-architect --persona-security

# Agent with language
--persona-scribe=fr

# Agent with other flags
--persona-performance --think-hard --uc

# Command with agent
/build --persona-backend --validate
```

For detailed information about each agent, click on the agent name in the tables above to view their full specification.

## Agent Teams Integration

The persona-based agent system complements Claude Code's native **Agent Teams** feature (experimental). Agent Teams spawn separate Claude Code instances that communicate via peer-to-peer messaging, while personas operate within a single session.

### When to Use Each
| Scenario | Best Approach |
|----------|--------------|
| Single-session domain expertise | Personas (`--persona-*` flags) |
| Parallel same-repo collaboration | Agent Teams (teammates with shared task list) |
| Isolated multi-branch work | `gwt-parallel` (devcontainer + worktree per branch) |
| Autonomous ticket execution | `gwt-ticket` + OpenCode + nvim |

### Combining Personas with Agent Teams
Teammates spawned via Agent Teams load CLAUDE.md, so persona auto-activation works within each teammate's session. When creating teams, leverage this by assigning domain-specific context:
```
Spawn a teammate focused on security review (will auto-activate security persona)
Spawn a teammate for frontend components (will auto-activate frontend persona)
```

</system-reminder>

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
# SessionStart hook: Report available LSP servers to Claude Code context
# This injects a system message so Claude knows which LSP capabilities are active.
# Lightweight: only checks PATH for binaries, no network calls.

# LSP binary → language mapping

1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # work-detect.sh - SessionStart hook to detect active ralph-loop work
3: #
4: # If a ralph-loop or ticket-execute session is active in the current project,
5: # outputs resume context so Claude picks up where it left off.
6: # Silent exit (no output) when no active work is detected.
7: 
8: set -euo pipefail
9: 
10: # Parse YAML frontmatter value from a state file
11: parse_yaml() {
12:     local key="$1" file="$2"
13:     grep "^${key}:" "$file" 2>/dev/null | head -1 | sed "s/^${key}: *//" | tr -d '"'
14: }
15: 
16: # Determine project root - prefer CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR, fall back to git root
17: PROJECT_DIR="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null || echo "")}"
18: [[ -z "$PROJECT_DIR" ]] && exit 0
19: 
20: # Clear stale stuck-detection iteration file for this worktree.
21: # Without this, restarting a session in the same worktree inherits the old
22: # timestamp, making agent-state.sh think the new session is already stuck.
23: ITER_FILE="/tmp/tmux-claude-state/ralph-iter-$(echo "$PROJECT_DIR" | tr '/' '-')"
24: rm -f "$ITER_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
25: 
26: RALPH_FILE="$PROJECT_DIR/.claude/ralph-loop.local.md"
27: TICKET_FILE="$PROJECT_DIR/.claude/ticket-execute.local.md"
28: 
29: # Check ralph-loop state first, then ticket-execute as fallback
30: ACTIVE=""
31: STATE_FILE=""
32: 
33: if [[ -f "$RALPH_FILE" ]] && [[ "$(parse_yaml active "$RALPH_FILE")" == "true" ]]; then
34:     ACTIVE=1
35:     STATE_FILE="$RALPH_FILE"
36: elif [[ -f "$TICKET_FILE" ]] && [[ "$(parse_yaml active "$TICKET_FILE")" == "true" ]]; then
37:     ACTIVE=1
38:     STATE_FILE="$TICKET_FILE"
39: fi
40: 
41: # Silent exit if no active work
42: [[ -z "$ACTIVE" ]] && exit 0
43: 
44: # Extract state info
45: ITERATION=$(parse_yaml iteration "$STATE_FILE")
46: MAX_ITERATIONS=$(parse_yaml max_iterations "$STATE_FILE")
47: ISSUE_KEY=$(parse_yaml issue_key "$TICKET_FILE" 2>/dev/null)
48: TITLE=$(parse_yaml title "$TICKET_FILE" 2>/dev/null)
49: 
50: # Build iteration string
51: ITER_STR=""
52: if [[ -n "$ITERATION" ]]; then
53:     ITER_STR="Iteration: ${ITERATION}"
54:     [[ -n "$MAX_ITERATIONS" ]] && ITER_STR="${ITER_STR}/${MAX_ITERATIONS}"
55:     ITER_STR="${ITER_STR}."
56: fi
57: 
58: # Build issue string
59: ISSUE_STR=""
60: if [[ -n "$ISSUE_KEY" ]]; then
61:     ISSUE_STR="${ISSUE_KEY}"
62:     [[ -n "$TITLE" ]] && ISSUE_STR="${ISSUE_STR} '${TITLE}'"
63: else
64:     [[ -n "$TITLE" ]] && ISSUE_STR="'${TITLE}'"
65: fi
66: 
67: # Seance: check for crash-recovery predecessor context (written by worktree-witness on_crash_retry)
68: # If present, output it prominently and consume it (ephemeral wisp pattern).
69: SEANCE_FILE="$PROJECT_DIR/.claude/seance-crash.md"
70: if [[ -f "$SEANCE_FILE" ]]; then
71:     cat "$SEANCE_FILE"
72:     rm -f "$SEANCE_FILE"
73:     exit 0 # Seance context is complete — skip normal work-detect output
74: fi
75: 
76: # Try to get latest checkpoint context via entire CLI (fast, local-only)
77: # PERF: Added 5s timeout to prevent blocking SessionStart on slow git operations
78: CKPT_SUMMARY=""
79: if command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then
80:     CKPT_SUMMARY=$(timeout 5 entire resume 2>/dev/null | head -20) || true
81: fi
82: 
83: # Check for active molecule state
84: # PERF: Added 5s timeout to prevent blocking SessionStart on slow molecule operations
85: MOL_SUMMARY=""
86: MOLECULE_ID=$(parse_yaml "molecule_id" "$TICKET_FILE" 2>/dev/null)
87: if [[ -n "$MOLECULE_ID" ]]; then
88:     MOL_SCRIPT="$HOME/dotfiles/scripts/molecule.sh"
89:     [[ -x "$MOL_SCRIPT" ]] || MOL_SCRIPT="$HOME/dotfiles-gastown/scripts/molecule.sh"
90:     if [[ -x "$MOL_SCRIPT" ]]; then
91:         MOL_SUMMARY=$(timeout 5 "$MOL_SCRIPT" resume "$MOLECULE_ID" 2>/dev/null | head -10) || true
92:     fi
93: fi
94: 
95: # Harness self-improvement: check for HIGH priority suggestions
96: # Silent when harness is healthy — only injects context when action is needed.
97: HARNESS_SUGGESTIONS=""
98: SUGGEST_SCRIPT="$PROJECT_DIR/scripts/harness/suggest-improvements.sh"
99: if [[ -x "$SUGGEST_SCRIPT" ]]; then
100:     HARNESS_JSON=$(timeout 5 "$SUGGEST_SCRIPT" --json 2>/dev/null) || true
101:     if [[ -n "$HARNESS_JSON" ]] && [[ "$HARNESS_JSON" != "[]" ]]; then
102:         # Only surface HIGH priority items to the agent
103:         HIGH_ITEMS=$(echo "$HARNESS_JSON" | jq -r '.[] | select(.priority == "HIGH") | "- [\(.category)] \(.suggestion) → \(.action)"' 2>/dev/null) || true
104:         if [[ -n "$HIGH_ITEMS" ]]; then
105:             HARNESS_SUGGESTIONS="HARNESS IMPROVEMENTS NEEDED: Before completing your task, fix these HIGH priority issues and commit the changes: $HIGH_ITEMS"
106:         fi
107:     fi
108: fi
109: 
110: # Build resume message
111: MSG="RESUME CONTEXT: Working on ${ISSUE_STR:-(unknown task)}."
112: [[ -n "$ITER_STR" ]] && MSG="$MSG $ITER_STR"
113: [[ -n "$MOL_SUMMARY" ]] && MSG="$MSG Molecule: $MOL_SUMMARY"
114: [[ -n "$CKPT_SUMMARY" ]] && MSG="$MSG Checkpoint: $CKPT_SUMMARY"
115: [[ -n "$HARNESS_SUGGESTIONS" ]] && MSG="$MSG $HARNESS_SUGGESTIONS"
116: MSG="$MSG Use /ralph-loop:ralph-loop to continue or review the current state."
117: 
118: echo "$MSG"
119: exit 0

(End of file - total 119 lines)
</content>

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# changelog-resume.sh - Emit recent changelog entries at SessionStart

set -euo pipefail

PROJECT_DIR="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null || echo "")}" 
[[ -z "$PROJECT_DIR" ]] && exit 0

CHANGELOG_FILE="$PROJECT_DIR/.claude/CHANGELOG.md"
[[ -f "$CHANGELOG_FILE" ]] || exit 0

ENTRIES=$(grep -E '^\[.*\] (PROGRESS|DECISION|FAILED|METRIC|DISCOVERY):' "$CHANGELOG_FILE" 2>/dev/null | tail -10 || true)
[[ -n "$ENTRIES" ]] || exit 0

cat <<EOF

$ENTRIES
=== End Session Changelog ===
EOF

parts.text:
Most likely causes, in order:

1. `.config/opencode/opencode.json:69` has `"lsp": true`.
   This was added in commit `2142ba07 Enable Opencode LSPs`. OpenCode now starts language servers on open. In large repos, especially Terraform-heavy ones, `terraform-ls` can crawl huge module trees and emit lots of diagnostics. Your logs show repeated `terraform-ls` startup/scanning activity.

2. Your current uncommitted config added `./plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts`.
   That wrapper imports the global `opencode-with-claude` plugin and starts/reuses a Meridian Claude proxy. That can add startup latency, especially if Claude/Meridian auth or proxy initialization is slow.

3. `./plugin/claude-compat.ts` does synchronous SessionStart work.
   On `session.created`, it waits for:
   `fix-hookify-imports.sh`, `plugin-chmod-fix.sh`, tmux start hook, dream count, `bd prime`, then `work-detect.sh`, `lsp-status.sh`, `plan-resume.sh`, and `changelog-resume.sh`.
   `bd prime` and any project resume logic can make opening feel blocked.

4. OpenCode server logs show `MaxListenersExceededWarning`.
   That suggests plugin/server listener accumulation. Not necessarily the main startup delay, but it points at plugin overhead.

Fastest confirmation path:

1. Temporarily set `"lsp": false` and restart OpenCode.
2. If still slow, remove `./plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts` from the plugin list and restart.
3. If still slow, profile/defer the `claude-compat.ts` SessionStart hooks, especially `bd prime`.

I’m currently read-only, so I didn’t change anything. If you want, I can apply the minimal fix next: disable OpenCode LSP by default and make heavy SessionStart context collection non-blocking or opt-in.

3. ./plugin/claude-compat.ts does synchronous SessionStart work.
On session.created, it waits for:
fix-hookify-imports.sh, plugin-chmod-fix.sh, tmux start hook, 
dream count, bd prime, then work-detect.sh, lsp-status.sh, plan-
resume.sh, and changelog-resume.sh.
bd prime and any project resume logic can make opening feel 
blocked. 

Is there a way to improve this behaviour?

Yes. The best improvement is to make OpenCode startup return immediately and shift non-critical context gathering into background/deferred paths.

Recommended changes:

1. Make SessionStart context collection non-blocking
   In `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts`, `session.created` currently does:

   ```ts
   await collectSessionStartContext()
   ```

   Change the behavior to fire-and-forget:

   ```ts
   void collectSessionStartContext().catch(() => {})
   ```

   Result: OpenCode opens immediately, and context trickles in shortly after.

2. Split startup hooks by priority
   Keep only cheap, UI-critical work synchronous:

   ```ts
   tmux-agent-start.sh
   ```

   Move these to async/deferred:

   ```text
   fix-hookify-imports.sh
   plugin-chmod-fix.sh
   dream/count-session.sh
   bd prime
   work-detect.sh
   lsp-status.sh
   plan-resume.sh
   changelog-resume.sh
   ```

3. Add timeouts around expensive subprocesses
   `bd prime`, `plan-resume.sh`, `work-detect.sh`, and `changelog-resume.sh` should never be allowed to block OpenCode startup indefinitely. A small timeout like `1500-3000ms` is enough.

4. Cache stable context per project
   `bd prime` and LSP status do not need to run every launch. Cache their output under something like:

   ```text
   ~/.cache/opencode/session-context/<project-hash>.json
   ```

   Refresh in background if stale, but inject cached context immediately.

5. Make heavy startup context opt-in
   Add env flags:

   ```text
   OPENCODE_STARTUP_CONTEXT=light|full|off
   OPENCODE_BD_PRIME_DISABLE=1
   OPENCODE_PLAN_RESUME_DISABLE=1
   ```

   Default should be `light`.

6. Defer context injection until first prompt
   Instead of collecting everything on `session.created`, run some context gathering on first `message.part.updated` for a user prompt. That shifts cost from “opening OpenCode” to “first actual interaction”, and only when needed.

Best minimal fix:

```ts
case "session.created": {
  currentSessionID = event.properties.info.id
  shutdownHandled = false
  sessionContext.clear()
  transientContext.length = 0
  seenPromptMessages.clear()
  messageRoles.clear()
  messageTexts.clear()
  messageOrder.length = 0
  lastBridgeReviewedAssistant = ""
  void collectSessionStartContext().catch(() => {})
  break
}
```

I’d implement that first, then add per-hook timeouts if startup is still sluggish.
Make these changes
I’ll make the OpenCode SessionStart path non-blocking first, then add bounded timeouts around the startup context subprocesses so slow local tools cannot hold the TUI open.

content.summary:
User asked: "Why is my opencode taking so long to open now?" Investigation loaded `customize-opencode` skill because this concerns OpenCode configuration. Read-only inspection found current repo `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles` with uncommitted OpenCode-related changes: `.config/opencode/opencode.json`, `scripts/opencode/doctor.sh`, `scripts/setup.sh`, `scripts/test-filter.sh`, and untracked `.opencode/plugins/`. Recent git log included `2142ba07 Enable Opencode LSPs`, `8800dce4 Opencode fixes`, `6a2abe04 Opencode fixes`, etc.

Important config findings from `.config/opencode/opencode.json`:
- `model`: `openai/gpt-5.5`
- `small_model`: `anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5`
- `plugin` list currently includes `@tarquinen/opencode-dcp@latest`, `./plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts`, `./plugin/claude-compat.ts`, `./plugin/session-env.ts`, `./plugin/nvim-open.ts`.
- `lsp`: `true` at line 69.
- `watcher.ignore` excludes `node_modules/**`, `dist/**`, `.git/**`, `*.pyc`, `__pycache__/**`, `.direnv/**`, `.beads/**`, `.entire/**`.

Diff showed uncommitted addition of `./plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts` to OpenCode plugin list, related doctor/setup/test updates, and test change expecting the subscription plugin configured. `git show 2142ba07` confirmed commit `Enable Opencode LSPs` was exactly a one-line addition of `"lsp": true` to `.config/opencode/opencode.json`.

Key plugin findings:
- `.config/opencode/plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts` imports global plugin at `$HOME/.bun/install/global/node_modules/opencode-with-claude/dist/index.js`, seeds Meridian profiles if missing, and memoizes a shared `ClaudeMaxPlugin(input)` promise so only one upstream proxy plugin is started per run. This can add startup latency if Meridian/Claude proxy initialization is slow.
- `.config/opencode/plugin/session-env.ts` is lightweight: tracks session ID/model and exposes `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID`/`OPENCODE_MODEL` in shell env.
- `.config/opencode/plugin/project-env.ts` is lightweight: sets `PROJECT_ROOT`, `REPO_ROOT`, `DOTFILES_ROOT`, `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` in shell env.
- `.config/opencode/plugin/entire.ts` hooks OpenCode events to `entire hooks opencode ...`; not in current plugin list.
- `.config/opencode/plugin/nvim-open.ts` only runs after write/edit/multiedit/patch/apply_patch in tmux, checks nvim sockets, and opens edited files; not startup-heavy.
- `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts` was the central startup-cost concern. On `session.created`, it reset state and awaited `collectSessionStartContext()`. `collectSessionStartContext()` ran in parallel: `scripts/fix-hookify-imports.sh`, `.claude/hooks/plugin-chmod-fix.sh`, `scripts/tmux/hooks/tmux-agent-start.sh`, `.claude/skills/dream/count-session.sh`, `bd prime`; then sequentially ran context scripts `.claude/hooks/work-detect.sh`, `lsp-status.sh`, `plan-resume.sh`, `changelog-resume.sh`. These subprocesses could make opening feel blocked.

Relevant hook details inspected:
- `.claude/hooks/lsp-status.sh`: cheap; checks PATH for LSP binaries (`pyright-langserver`, `typescript-language-server`, `gopls`, `rust-analyzer`, `bash-language-server`, `yaml-language-server`, `terraform-ls`, `lua-language-server`, `nil`) and prints active LSP message.
- `.claude/hooks/work-detect.sh`: can do resume detection; removes stale `/tmp/tmux-claude-state/ralph-iter-*`, checks `.claude/ralph-loop.local.md` and `.claude/ticket-execute.local.md`; has internal `timeout 5` around `entire resume`, molecule resume, and harness suggestions.
- `.claude/hooks/plan-resume.sh`: auto-creates `.plan.md` if missing and `.claude/` exists, then extracts sections via `plan-extract-sections.sh` and prints living plan context.
- `.claude/hooks/changelog-resume.sh`: greps last 10 changelog entries from `.claude/CHANGELOG.md`.
- `scripts/tmux/hooks/tmux-agent-start.sh`: cheap tmux window color update only when `TMUX_AGENT_TARGET` is set.

Log findings:
- `~/.local/state/opencode/serve.out.log` had only repeated `opencode server listening on http://127.0.0.1:4096`.
- `~/.local/state/opencode/serve.err.log` had `MaxListenersExceededWarning: Possible EventTarget memory leak detected. 11 event listeners added to [DL]`, suggesting plugin/server listener accumulation or overhead.
- Grep in `~/.local/state` found huge Neovim LSP logs with repeated `terraform-ls` startup/scanning/diagnostic activity in `/Users/shahe.islam/work/aws-account-bootstrapping`, including many modules and stack traces. This supported that OpenCode `lsp: true` can make large Terraform repos slow/noisy.

Initial answer to user identified likely causes in order:
1. `.config/opencode/opencode.json:69` has `"lsp": true`; added by commit `2142ba07 Enable Opencode LSPs`; in Terraform-heavy repos `terraform-ls` can crawl huge module trees.
2. Current uncommitted config added `./plugin/opencode-with-claude.ts`, importing global `opencode-with-claude` and starting/reusing Meridian Claude proxy.
3. `./plugin/claude-compat.ts` synchronously awaited SessionStart context work including `bd prime` and resume hooks.
4. OpenCode server logs show `MaxListenersExceededWarning`.
Recommended quick confirmations: set `lsp` false, remove `opencode-with-claude`, then profile/defer `claude-compat.ts` startup hooks.

User then quoted the SessionStart finding and asked: "Is there a way to improve this behaviour?" Response recommended:
- Make `session.created` fire-and-forget with `void collectSessionStartContext().catch(() => {})` instead of `await collectSessionStartContext()`.
- Split startup hooks by priority: keep `tmux-agent-start.sh` synchronous/cheap; move `fix-hookify-imports.sh`, `plugin-chmod-fix.sh`, dream count, `bd prime`, `work-detect.sh`, `lsp-status.sh`, `plan-resume.sh`, and `changelog-resume.sh` async/deferred.
- Add `1500-3000ms` timeouts around expensive subprocesses (`bd prime`, `plan-resume.sh`, `work-detect.sh`, `changelog-resume.sh`).
- Cache stable context per project under e.g. `~/.cache/opencode/session-context/<project-hash>.json` in future.
- Make heavy startup context opt-in with env flags like `OPENCODE_STARTUP_CONTEXT=light|full|off`, `OPENCODE_BD_PRIME_DISABLE=1`, `OPENCODE_PLAN_RESUME_DISABLE=1`, default `light`.
- Optionally defer some context until first prompt.
Best minimal code snippet given was replacing `await collectSessionStartContext()` with `void collectSessionStartContext().catch(() => {})` in `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts`.

state.title: Show working tree changes
Review final startup context diff

parts.text:
Implemented the OpenCode startup improvements.

Changed:
- `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts`
  - `session.created` no longer blocks on full startup context.
  - Keeps only `tmux-agent-start.sh` synchronous with a `500ms` cap.
  - Runs context collection fire-and-forget.
  - Adds `2500ms` subprocess timeouts.
  - Defaults `OPENCODE_STARTUP_CONTEXT` to `light`.
  - Makes `bd prime` and dream count opt-in via `OPENCODE_STARTUP_CONTEXT=full`.
  - Supports:
    - `OPENCODE_STARTUP_CONTEXT=off|light|full`
    - `OPENCODE_BD_PRIME_DISABLE=1`
    - `OPENCODE_DREAM_DISABLE=1`
    - `OPENCODE_PLAN_RESUME_DISABLE=1`
    - `OPENCODE_CHANGELOG_RESUME_DISABLE=1`

- `scripts/opencode/test-claude-compat.sh`
  - Updated the harness to wait briefly for async SessionStart context instead of assuming immediate injection.

Validation:
- `bun --check .config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts` passed.
- `scripts/opencode/test-claude-compat.sh` passed.
- `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` passed: `185/185`.

Note: OpenCode loads config/plugins at startup, so quit and restart OpenCode to pick this up.
That's much faster, based on that feedback are there any furhter optimisations to make?

**Inspecting current files**

I'm currently in read-only mode, so I should find an answer by inspecting the current file. I might use some read or grep functions to help me with that. I don’t need to make any edits right now, but perhaps I could ask if there's anything specific I need to look for? It might also be a good idea to check the relevant plugin after any changes have been made.
I’ll review the remaining startup path read-only and identify optimizations that are likely to help without trading away useful context.

1: import { existsSync } from "node:fs"

5:   ClaudeMaxPlugin: Plugin
6: }
7: 
8: let sharedPluginPromise: Promise<Awaited<ReturnType<Plugin>>> | null = null
9: 
10: function seedMeridianProfile(home: string) {
11:   if (process.env.MERIDIAN_PROFILES) {
12:     return
13:   }
14: 
15:   const profilesFile = `${home}/.config/meridian/profiles.json`
16:   if (existsSync(profilesFile)) {
17:     return
18:   }
19: 
20:   process.env.MERIDIAN_PROFILES = JSON.stringify([
21:     {
22:       id: "default",
23:       claudeConfigDir: `${home}/.claude`,
24:     },
25:   ])
26: 
27:   if (!process.env.MERIDIAN_DEFAULT_PROFILE) {
28:     process.env.MERIDIAN_DEFAULT_PROFILE = "default"
29:   }
30: }
31: 
32: async function loadSharedPlugin(input: Parameters<Plugin>[0]) {
33:   if (!sharedPluginPromise) {
34:     sharedPluginPromise = (async () => {
35:       const home = process.env.HOME
36:       if (!home) {
37:         throw new Error("HOME is required to load opencode-with-claude")
38:       }
39: 
40:       seedMeridianProfile(home)
41: 
42:       // OpenCode can initialize plugins multiple times in one process while it
43:       // bootstraps worker/session phases. Reuse a single upstream plugin
44:       // instance so Meridian only starts one Claude proxy per run.
45:       const modulePath = `${home}/.bun/install/global/node_modules/opencode-with-claude/dist/index.js`
46:       const { ClaudeMaxPlugin } = (await import(modulePath)) as ClaudePluginModule
47:       return ClaudeMaxPlugin(input)
48:     })().catch((error) => {
49:       sharedPluginPromise = null
50:       throw error
51:     })
52:   }
53: 
54:   return sharedPluginPromise
55: }
56: 
57: const OpencodeWithClaudePlugin: Plugin = async (input) => {
58:   return loadSharedPlugin(input)
59: }
60: 
61: export default OpencodeWithClaudePlugin

1: // OpenCode plugin: expose session metadata to shell commands.
2: // Tmux window colors are pane-local and owned by scripts/opencode/tmux-open.sh.
3: import type { Plugin } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
4: 
5: export const SessionEnvPlugin: Plugin = async () => {
6:   let currentSessionID: string | null = null
7:   let currentModel: string | null = null
8: 
9:   return {
10:     event: async ({ event }) => {
11:       switch (event.type) {
12:         case "session.created": {
13:           const session = (event as any).properties?.info
14:           if (!session?.id) break
15:           currentSessionID = session.id
16:           break
17:         }
18: 
19:         case "message.updated": {
20:           const msg = (event as any).properties?.info
21:           if (msg?.role === "assistant" && msg?.modelID) {
22:             currentModel = msg.modelID
23:           }
24:           break
25:         }
26: 
27:         case "session.deleted": {
28:           currentSessionID = null
29:           currentModel = null
30:           break
31:         }
32: 
33:         case "server.instance.disposed": {
34:           // OpenCode can dispose transient server instances while the attached
35:           // TUI is still alive. Session metadata is cleared only on deletion.
36:           break
37:         }
38:       }
39:     },
40:     "shell.env": async (input, output) => {
41:       const sessionID = input.sessionID ?? currentSessionID
42:       if (sessionID) {
43:         currentSessionID = sessionID
44:         output.env.OPENCODE_SESSION_ID = sessionID
45:       }
46:       if (currentModel) {
47:         output.env.OPENCODE_MODEL = currentModel
48:       }
49:     },
50:   }
51: }
52:

parts.text:
**Restarting server after updates**

I need to restart the shared server after making changes to the plugins. If the issue persists afterward, I should take a closer look at the profile settings or configurations. It’s essential to ensure everything is running smoothly, so I want to keep track of any changes made during this process. If something goes wrong, I'll have to troubleshoot further. I’m hopeful a restart will do the trick!

Yes. The biggest win is done, but there are a few sensible next optimizations.

Recommended next changes, in order:

1. Disable or scope OpenCode LSP
   `lsp: true` is still the likely biggest remaining startup/background cost in Terraform-heavy repos. I’d either set global `lsp` to `false`, or keep it disabled globally and enable it only in specific project `.opencode/opencode.json` files.

2. Add watcher ignores for infra/vendor caches
   Add ignores like `.terraform/**`, `**/.terraform/**`, `.terragrunt-cache/**`, `**/.terragrunt-cache/**`, `.venv/**`, `.mypy_cache/**`, `.pytest_cache/**`. This is low-risk and should reduce file watching churn.

3. Parallelize deferred context scripts
   `work-detect.sh`, `lsp-status.sh`, `plan-resume.sh`, and `changelog-resume.sh` currently run sequentially after startup. They no longer block opening, but parallelizing them would make context arrive faster and reduce background tail latency.

4. Stop running maintenance scripts every session
   `fix-hookify-imports.sh` and `plugin-chmod-fix.sh` are hygiene tasks. Better behavior would be TTL-gated, for example once per day, or moved to `opencode doctor` / setup. They should not run every new session unless needed.

5. Make `opencode-with-claude` opt-in or conditional
   That plugin still initializes at OpenCode plugin load. If you mostly use `openai/gpt-5.5`, it may be unnecessary startup work. Options:
   - Keep it only when using Anthropic subscription models.
   - Gate it behind an env var.
   - Remove it from default plugin list and expose a separate wrapper/config for Claude-subscription mode.

6. Add lightweight startup timing logs
   Add debug-only timing around plugin init, SessionStart hooks, LSP startup, and subscription proxy init. That would prevent guessing next time and make regressions obvious.

My suggested next patch would be small:
- Add Terraform/cache watcher ignores.
- Parallelize deferred SessionStart context scripts.
- TTL-gate `fix-hookify-imports.sh` and `plugin-chmod-fix.sh`.
- Leave `lsp` and `opencode-with-claude` as a user choice because those affect features, not just performance.

1. Disable or scope OpenCode LSP
lsp: true is still the likely biggest remaining startup/
background cost in Terraform-heavy repos. I’d either set global 
lsp to false, or keep it disabled globally and enable it only 
in specific project .opencode/opencode.json files. Isnt having LSPs always enabled a net positive?

Not always. LSPs are a net positive when their indexing cost is bounded and their signal is used. They become a net negative when they eagerly scan huge/generated/vendor trees or attach servers you do not need for the current task.

For your setup, the concern is specifically Terraform-heavy repos. `terraform-ls` often crawls modules, provider schemas, `.terraform`, Terragrunt caches, and nested stacks. That can create real startup/background drag even if OpenCode itself opens quickly.

The better framing is:

- Always-on LSPs are good for small/medium codebases where diagnostics and symbols are immediately useful.
- Always-on LSPs are bad for repos with huge generated/cache trees, many Terraform modules, monorepos, or languages with expensive indexers.
- Scoped LSPs are usually best: enable globally only if most repos benefit, otherwise enable per project where the cost is justified.

I would not say “turn LSP off forever.” I’d say:

1. Keep LSP enabled for normal app repos.
2. Disable it for known-problem repos like large Terraform/IaC workspaces.
3. Add watcher/LSP ignores for `.terraform/**`, `**/.terraform/**`, `.terragrunt-cache/**`, `**/.terragrunt-cache/**`, and other generated caches first.
4. If startup/background load is still high, set global `lsp: false` and opt in with project `.opencode/opencode.json`.

For your dotfiles global config, `lsp: true` means every OpenCode session pays the possibility of language-server startup. That is only a net positive if most sessions need that. Given you already use Neovim LSP heavily, OpenCode LSP is probably most valuable for agent-side code awareness, but less valuable than a responsive TUI.

My recommendation: keep LSP enabled for now, add aggressive Terraform/cache ignores, and only scope/disable LSP if the slowness returns in infra repos.

3. Parallelize deferred context scripts
work-detect.sh, lsp-status.sh, plan-resume.sh, and changelog-
resume.sh currently run sequentially after startup. They no 
longer block opening, but parallelizing them would make context 
arrive faster and reduce background tail latency.

4. Stop running maintenance scripts every session
fix-hookify-imports.sh and plugin-chmod-fix.sh are hygiene 
tasks. Better behavior would be TTL-gated, for example once per 
day, or moved to opencode doctor / setup. They should not run 
every new session unless needed. These two points seem appropriate to implement
I’ll implement both in `claude-compat.ts`: make deferred context hooks run concurrently, and add a small cache/TTL gate so maintenance hooks run at most once per day per project.

## Conventions

**Considering script modifications**

I’m thinking about using Promise.all to run the scripts in order. I can modify runContextScript or just add a collection. It’s pretty simple. I'll set up contextScripts as an array and push optional items. By using await with Promise.all, I can run each script and then add the results with addContextResult. I wonder if we still need runContextScript or if I should modify it to return the result? Let's patch this up!

I’m going to keep context ordering stable while still parallelizing: launch the resume scripts together, then inject their outputs in the original order after they finish. Maintenance stamps will live under `~/.cache/opencode/claude-compat-maintenance/`.

parts.text:
**Evaluating file path normalization**

I’m thinking about how `mkdirSync(join(stampPath, ".."))` doesn’t normalize as expected. If I do `join('/a/file', '..')`, it returns `/a`, which is fine. However, if the path includes `file.stamp/..`, it works differently. I should consider using `dirname` for clarity. If `stampPath` doesn’t exist, creating `/cache/hash/file.stamp/..` might create `/cache/hash`, and I suppose Node handles normalization well. I should check on `runContextScript`, as it might be unused after running in parallel.

state.title: Show current repository status

parts.text:
**Analyzing git status**

I'm seeing that the git status shows only our file is tracked, plus an untracked .opencode/plugins folder. I’m wondering if there were previous modifications that might be missing — maybe the user changed something? It looks like some files haven’t been touched. I might need to run without committing for now. Also, I think it could be cleaner to use dirname. Perhaps I need to consider patching it and running grep.

state.title: runContextScript\(

parts.text:
**Considering code cleanup**

I see that the unused function should be removed to make the code cleaner. It’s always great to streamline things! I also need to use the `dirname` import — that seems like a useful addition. I guess I'll apply those changes and create a patch for it. I'm curious how the final result will improve the project's overall readability and efficiency. Keeping code tidy is definitely important!

input.patchText:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts
@@
-import { join } from "node:path"
+import { dirname, join } from "node:path"
@@
-  async function runContextScript(script: string, timeoutMs = 0) {
-    const result = await runScript(script, undefined, {}, timeoutMs)
-    addContextResult(result)
-  }
-
   function addContextResult(result: HookResult) {
@@
     }
 
     try {
-      mkdirSync(join(stampPath, ".."), { recursive: true })
+      mkdirSync(dirname(stampPath), { recursive: true })
       writeFileSync(stampPath, new Date().toISOString(), "utf8")
*** End Patch

parts.text:
**Deciding on final actions**

I’m considering how to finalize my work. Should I mention anything untracked, and should I check the status with git? I think I need to finalize with tests but don’t necessarily need to push unless the user asks for it. However, according to instructions, I do need to push when finished. The developer indicated that I only need to commit or push if explicitly requested, so maybe no push is required after all. Keeping it concise sounds good!

Implemented both optimizations in `.config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts`.

Changed:
- Deferred context scripts now run in parallel:
  - `work-detect.sh`
  - `lsp-status.sh`
  - `plan-resume.sh`
  - `changelog-resume.sh`
- Context output is still injected in stable order after parallel execution.
- `fix-hookify-imports.sh` and `plugin-chmod-fix.sh` are now TTL-gated.
- Maintenance hooks run at most once every 24 hours per project.
- TTL stamps live under `~/.cache/opencode/claude-compat-maintenance/<project-hash>/`.
- Added `OPENCODE_MAINTENANCE_DISABLE=1` to skip those hygiene hooks entirely.

Validation passed:
- `bun --check .config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts`
- `scripts/opencode/test-claude-compat.sh`
- `scripts/test-filter.sh opencode` → `185/185`

OpenCode needs a restart to load the updated plugin.

Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
	modified:   .config/opencode/plugin/claude-compat.ts

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	.opencode/plugins/

no changes added to commit (use "git add" and/or "git commit -a")
❯ git add .
❯ git commit -m "speed up opencode"
Harness pre-commit: checking staged files...

Pre-commit: all checks passed
[main 83d55325] speed up opencode
 1 file changed, 57 insertions(+), 10 deletions(-)
❯ git status
On branch main
Your branch is ahead of 'origin/main' by 2 commits.
  (use "git push" to publish your local commits)

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	.opencode/plugins/

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)

~/dotfiles➜ 

Why is this untracked?

**Inspecting untracked changes**

I need to figure out why something is untracked. I should look into the opencode/plugins. Also, the user has committed our changes, so that’s important! I’m thinking I might run tools to help with this, and maybe customizing the opencode skill is necessary? I see that the opencode config is loaded in a compressed format, but I'm unsure if that's sufficient. I’ll inspect the status and directory to get clarity.
I’ll inspect that directory and the repo ignore rules to see whether it is generated plugin material that should be ignored or source that should be tracked.

parts.text: The directory is empty in my current view, and `git status --untracked-files=all` no longer reports it. That points to a transient/generated `.opencode/plugins/` directory rather than a file Git can commit.

`.opencode/plugins/` is untracked because it is not in `.gitignore`, and something created it locally.

Right now it appears empty, and Git does not track empty directories, so `git add .` did not include it in your commit. That is why the commit says only:

`1 file changed, 57 insertions(+), 10 deletions(-)`

Likely source: OpenCode/plugin tooling created `.opencode/plugins/` as a local runtime/cache/plugin directory.

Recommendation: ignore it if it is generated runtime state.

Add this to `.gitignore`:

```gitignore
.opencode/plugins/
```

Current status from my side no longer shows it, so it may have disappeared or only existed briefly, but adding the ignore would prevent it coming back in `git status`.

# Permissions

Control which actions require approval to run.

OpenCode uses the `permission` config to decide whether a given action should run automatically, prompt you, or be blocked.

As of `v1.1.1`, the legacy `tools` boolean config is deprecated and has been merged into `permission`. The old `tools` config is still supported for backwards compatibility.

---

## [Actions](#actions)

Each permission rule resolves to one of:

-   `"allow"` — run without approval
-   `"ask"` — prompt for approval
-   `"deny"` — block the action

---

## [Configuration](#configuration)

You can set permissions globally (with `*`), and override specific tools.

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": {    "*": "ask",    "bash": "allow",    "edit": "deny"  }}
```

You can also set all permissions at once:

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": "allow"}
```

---

## [Granular Rules (Object Syntax)](#granular-rules-object-syntax)

For most permissions, you can use an object to apply different actions based on the tool input.

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": {    "bash": {      "*": "ask",      "git *": "allow",      "npm *": "allow",      "rm *": "deny",      "grep *": "allow"    },    "edit": {      "*": "deny",      "packages/web/src/content/docs/*.mdx": "allow"    }  }}
```

Rules are evaluated by pattern match, with the **last matching rule winning**. A common pattern is to put the catch-all `"*"` rule first, and more specific rules after it.

### [Wildcards](#wildcards)

Permission patterns use simple wildcard matching:

-   `*` matches zero or more of any character
-   `?` matches exactly one character
-   All other characters match literally

### [Home Directory Expansion](#home-directory-expansion)

You can use `~` or `$HOME` at the start of a pattern to reference your home directory. This is particularly useful for [`external_directory`](#external-directories) rules.

-   `~/projects/*` -> `/Users/username/projects/*`
-   `$HOME/projects/*` -> `/Users/username/projects/*`
-   `~` -> `/Users/username`

### [External Directories](#external-directories)

Use `external_directory` to allow tool calls that touch paths outside the working directory where OpenCode was started. This applies to any tool that takes a path as input (for example `read`, `edit`, `glob`, `grep`, and many `bash` commands).

Home expansion (like `~/...`) only affects how a pattern is written. It does not make an external path part of the current workspace, so paths outside the working directory must still be allowed via `external_directory`.

For example, this allows access to everything under `~/projects/personal/`:

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": {    "external_directory": {      "~/projects/personal/**": "allow"    }  }}
```

Any directory allowed here inherits the same defaults as the current workspace. Since [`read` defaults to `allow`](#defaults), reads are also allowed for entries under `external_directory` unless overridden. -   `websearch` — web search (matches the query)
-   `external_directory` — triggered when a tool touches paths outside the project working directory
-   `doom_loop` — triggered when the same tool call repeats 3 times with identical input

# Permissions

Control which actions require approval to run.

OpenCode uses the `permission` config to decide whether a given action should run automatically, prompt you, or be blocked.

As of `v1.1.1`, the legacy `tools` boolean config is deprecated and has been merged into `permission`. The old `tools` config is still supported for backwards compatibility.

---

## [Actions](#actions)

Each permission rule resolves to one of:

-   `"allow"` — run without approval
-   `"ask"` — prompt for approval
-   `"deny"` — block the action

---

## [Configuration](#configuration)

You can set permissions globally (with `*`), and override specific tools.

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": {    "*": "ask",    "bash": "allow",    "edit": "deny"  }}
```

You can also set all permissions at once:

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": "allow"}
```

---

## [Granular Rules (Object Syntax)](#granular-rules-object-syntax)

For most permissions, you can use an object to apply different actions based on the tool input.

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": {    "bash": {      "*": "ask",      "git *": "allow",      "npm *": "allow",      "rm *": "deny",      "grep *": "allow"    },    "edit": {      "*": "deny",      "packages/web/src/content/docs/*.mdx": "allow"    }  }}
```

Rules are evaluated by pattern match, with the **last matching rule winning**. A common pattern is to put the catch-all `"*"` rule first, and more specific rules after it.

### [Wildcards](#wildcards)

Permission patterns use simple wildcard matching:

-   `*` matches zero or more of any character
-   `?` matches exactly one character
-   All other characters match literally

### [Home Directory Expansion](#home-directory-expansion)

You can use `~` or `$HOME` at the start of a pattern to reference your home directory. This is particularly useful for [`external_directory`](#external-directories) rules.

-   `~/projects/*` -> `/Users/username/projects/*`
-   `$HOME/projects/*` -> `/Users/username/projects/*`
-   `~` -> `/Users/username`

### [External Directories](#external-directories)

Use `external_directory` to allow tool calls that touch paths outside the working directory where OpenCode was started. This applies to any tool that takes a path as input (for example `read`, `edit`, `glob`, `grep`, and many `bash` commands).

Home expansion (like `~/...`) only affects how a pattern is written. It does not make an external path part of the current workspace, so paths outside the working directory must still be allowed via `external_directory`.

For example, this allows access to everything under `~/projects/personal/`:

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": {    "external_directory": {      "~/projects/personal/**": "allow"    }  }}
```

Any directory allowed here inherits the same defaults as the current workspace. Since [`read` defaults to `allow`](#defaults), reads are also allowed for entries under `external_directory` unless overridden. Add explicit rules when a tool should be restricted in these paths, such as blocking edits while keeping reads:

opencode.json

```
{  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",  "permission": {    "external_directory": {      "~/projects/personal/**": "allow"    },    "edit": {      "~/projects/personal/**": "deny"    }  }}
```

Keep the list focused on trusted paths, and layer extra allow or deny rules as needed for other tools (for example `bash`).

---

## [Available Permissions](#available-permissions)

OpenCode permissions are keyed by tool name, plus a couple of safety guards:

-   `read` — reading a file (matches the file path)
-   `edit` — all file modifications (covers `edit`, `write`, `patch`)
-   `glob` — file globbing (matches the glob pattern)
-   `grep` — content search (matches the regex pattern)
-   `bash` — running shell commands (matches parsed commands like `git status --porcelain`)
-   `task` — launching subagents (matches the subagent type)
-   `skill` — loading a skill (matches the skill name)
-   `lsp` — running LSP queries (currently non-granular)
-   `question` — asking the user questions during execution
-   `webfetch` — fetching a URL (matches the URL)
-   `websearch` — web search (matches the query)
-   `external_directory` — triggered when a tool touches paths outside the project working directory
-   `doom_loop` — triggered when the same tool call repeats 3 times with identical input

parts.text:
Yes, it is worth enabling, but not as `true`.

OpenCode’s schema says `doom_loop` is a permission action, not a boolean. Valid values are:

`"allow"`, `"ask"`, `"deny"`

Given your config currently has:

```json
"permission": "allow"
```

that likely means repeated identical tool calls are also allowed without interruption. That weakens the safety guard.

Recommended config shape:

```json
"permission": {
  "*": "allow",
  "doom_loop": "ask"
}
```

Why `ask` over `deny`:
- `ask` catches accidental loops while still letting you approve a legitimate repeated command.
- `deny` is safer but may interrupt valid retry/debug loops.
- `allow` defeats the point of the guard.

I would not change anything else yet. This is a good safety/performance guard because it stops agents burning time on the same failed tool call repeatedly, without making normal OpenCode usage more restrictive.

Because we’re in plan/read-only mode, I haven’t edited the config.
